---
title: How to download data from CDEC
output: html_notebook
author: Heather Short
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

```{r, load packages}

# install packages if necessary
#install.packages("CDECRetrieve")

# load packages
library(CDECRetrieve)
library(tidyverse)

```

# The code below (lines 24 to 110) explains how to download CDEC data one sensor and station at a time.

### Jump to line 115 to download multiple sensors and stations at once

# --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# What are the start and end dates you are interested in?
```{r, assign dates}

start_date = "2024-12-01"
  
end_date = "2025-06-01"

```

# What station are you interested in?
```{r, assign station ID}

station_id = "FPT" # Freeport

```

# What sensors are you interested in?
```{r, assign sensor codes}

# 20 = discharge
# 21 = velocity
# 25 = temperature
# 221 = turbidity

sensor_id = "25" # temperature

```

# This chunk creates the function that will download CDEC data and bind it into a dataframe. 
### Do not edit this chunk.
```{r, CDEC Retrieve function}

get_cdec_data <- function(station, sensor, start_date, end_date) {
  
  df <- cdec_query(station = station, 
                   sensor = sensor,
                   start_date = start_date, 
                   end_date = end_date, 
                   dur_code = "E") %>% 
    
            mutate(station_id = station,
                   sensor_id = sensor)
  
           return(df)
} # end function
```

# Use the get_cdec_data function.
```{r, create dataframe}

# if you edited the first 3 chunks and assigned values to station_id, sensor_id, start_date, and end_date, 
# you should not need to edit this chunk 

downloaded_df = get_cdec_data(station_id, sensor_id, start_date, end_date)

```

# Clean up the new dataframe and add more information.
```{r, clean up}

clean_df = downloaded_df %>% 
  mutate(downloaded_from = "CDEC", # makes a new column to show that this data was downloaded from CDEC
         value = parameter_value, # copies the parameter_value column to "value" column
         
         # make sure to edit these to reflect the sensor_id you chose
         value_type = "temperature", 
         units = "deg C", 
         
         # edit this to reflect the station_id you chose
         location = "Freeport", 
         
         # edit this to reflect the dates you chose
         water_year = "2025") %>% 
  
  # selects and reorders columns
  select(downloaded_from, location_id, location, water_year, datetime, value, value_type, units) 

```

# Create a test plot
```{r, test plot}

ggplot(clean_df, aes(x = datetime, y = value, color = value_type)) + 
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2)

```

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# # The code below explains how to download multiple sensors and stations at a time.

# What are the start and end dates you are interested in?
```{r, assign dates}

start_date = "2024-12-01"
  
end_date = "2025-06-01"

```


# What stations and sensors are you interested in?
```{r, assign stations and sensors}

# create a lookup table for the stations and sensors of interest
stations_sensors = tibble::tribble(~station_id, ~sensor_id,
                                   "FPT",       "20", 
                                   "FPT",       "25", 
                                   "GES",       "20", 
                                   "GES",       "25") 

# create a lookup table to give the sensor_id meaning
sensor_lookup = tibble::tribble(~sensor_id, ~value_type,  ~units,
                                "20",      "Discharge",   "cfs",
                                "25",      "Temperature", "deg C")

# create a lookup table to give the station_id meaning
station_lookup = tibble::tribble(~station_id, ~location,
                                 "FPT",       "Freeport", 
                                 "GES",       "Georgiana Slough")

```

# This chunk creates the function that will download CDEC data and bind it into a dataframe. 
### Do not edit this chunk.
```{r, CDEC Retrieve function}

get_cdec_data <- function(station, sensor, start_date, end_date) {
  
  df <- cdec_query(station = station, 
                   sensor = sensor,
                   start_date = start_date, 
                   end_date = end_date, 
                   dur_code = "E") %>% 
    
            mutate(station_id = station,
                   sensor_id = sensor)
  
           return(df)
} # end function
```

```{r, create dataframe}

downloaded_df_2 = stations_sensors %>% 
  mutate(data = map2(station_id, sensor_id, 
                     ~ get_cdec_data(.x, .y, start_date, end_date))) %>% 
  select(-station_id, -sensor_id) %>% 
  unnest(data)

```

# Clean up the new dataframe and add more information.
```{r, clean up}

# bind dataframe with lookup tables
downloaded_df_2 = downloaded_df_2 %>% 
  left_join(sensor_lookup, by = "sensor_id") %>% 
  left_join(station_lookup, by = "station_id")

# clean up df
clean_df_2 = downloaded_df_2 %>% 
  mutate(downloaded_from = "CDEC", # makes a new column to show that this data was downloaded from CDEC
         value = parameter_value, # copies the parameter_value column to "value" column
         
         # edit this to reflect the dates you chose
         water_year = "2025") %>% 
  
  # selects and reorders columns
  select(downloaded_from, location_id, location, water_year, datetime, value, value_type, units) 

```

# Create a test plot
```{r, test plot}

ggplot(clean_df_2, aes(x = datetime, y = value, color = value_type)) + 
  facet_grid(rows = vars(value_type), cols = vars(location), scales = "free_y") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2)

```
